perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall notify the Disclosing Party immediately upon discovery of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information or any other breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party , its employees, Affiliates or Representatives and will cooperate with the Disclos ing Party in every reasonable way to assist the Disclosing Party to regain possession of the Confidential Information and prevent its further unauthorized use or disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall notify the Disclosing Party within 30 days upon discovery of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information or any other breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party , its employees, Affiliates or Representatives and will cooperate with the Disclos ing Party in every reasonable way to assist the Disclosing Party to regain possession of the Confidential Information and prevent its further unauthorized use or disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the notification of a data breach to 30 days contradicts data breach notification laws that require immediate or prompt notification to mitigate damages.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 26E of the PDPA requires organizations to notify the PDPC and affected individuals as soon as practicable when a data breach occurs that may result in significant harm to the affected individuals. Specifying a 30-day notification period contradicts this requirement for immediate notification.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: PDPC | Page Not Found Search Toggle search bar Toggle mobile menu Search Be aware of scammers impersonating as PDPC officers and report any suspicious calls to the Police. perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a 30-day delay for notifying the Disclosing Party about a data breach, while the Law Explanation states that Section 26E of the PDPA requires organizations to notify the PDPC and affected individuals 'as soon as practicable' when a data breach occurs that may result in significant harm. The introduction of a 30-day window directly contradicts the requirement for immediate or prompt notification. This falls under the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition, since a time-sensitive obligation in the contract does not align with legal requirements.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties specifically agree that the confidentiality obligations set forth in this Agreement shall rema in in effect for a period of five (5 ) years from the date of this Agreement , despite any termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties specifically agree that the confidentiality obligations set forth in this Agreement shall rema in in effect for a period of twenty (20 ) years from the date of this Agreement , despite any termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the confidentiality obligations to 20 years may be deemed unreasonably long and against public policy in some jurisdictions, especially if it restricts competition or innovation. It also might conflict with statutes of limitations on certain claims.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Singapore doesn't have specific laws invalidating contracts exceeding a certain duration for confidentiality, extending it to 20 years could be challenged as an unreasonable restraint of trade or against public policy under Singapore Contract Law principles, especially when balanced against the Limitation Act (Cap. 163), where many claims have shorter limitation periods. While the contract itself remains enforceable, the practical value of enforcing confidentiality after certain periods diminishes with time, creating a possible conflict in legal principles.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: ncil’ to strike out those words which would render the covenant ‘unreasonable.’ In doing so, however, the court will not go so far as to re-write the contractual bargain which had been reached by the contracting parties. SECTION 13 JUDICIAL REMEDIES FOR BREACH OF CONTRACT Judicial Remedies Contrasted with Self-help Remedies 8.13.1Following a breach of a condition of a contract, or where the breach causes one party to be deprived of substantially the whole of the benefit of the contract, the aggrieved party may elect to bring the contract to an end. When this happens, both the aggrieved party and the party-in-breach will be released from any outstanding obligations under the contract. This is said to be a ‘self-help’ remedy because the release is effected without the need for any court approval or intervention. 8.13.2 Where the aggrieved party has suffered financial losses as a result of the breach, or where release of the party-in-breach from outstanding obligations will cause financial loss, discharge of contract alone may not be an adequate remedy. Recourse to other judicial remedies may be needed. Types of Judicial Remedies 8.13.3 In relation to contract law, the following types of judicial remedy are commonly sought: (a) the common law remedy of damages; (b) the common law remedy of an action for a fixed sum; (c) the equitable remedy of specific performance; and (d) the equitable remedy of injunction. It is important to draw the distinction between the common law and the equitable remedies because, while the former are available as of right, the latter are discretionary. Availability of Judicial Remedies – Time bars, Limitation Periods and Laches 8.13.4 Urgency should be the order of the day when seeking judicial remedies as access to judicial remedies may be barred by lapse of time. 8.13.5 Generally speaking, no action may be brought for a breach of contract after 6 years have lapsed from the time when the contract was breached – s 6 of the Limitation Act (Cap 163, 1996 Rev Ed). This bars access to the court insofar as the remedies of damages or an action for a fixed sum are concerned. [See Chapter 2 on Court Procedure for a fuller discussion]. 8.13.6 In relation to the equitable remedies of specific performance and injunction, the equitable doctrine of laches applies. Shortly put, applicants who delay applying for equitable relief from the courts may be turned away if the delay is inordinate and inexcusable, such that it would be inequitable to grant such relief. Indeed, an application for an order for specific performance might be denied if the application is not made as soon as the nature of the case might permit. Damages – Compensation for Pecuniary Loss 8.13.7 Contractual damages are awarded to an aggrieved party in the form of a sum of money, in compensation for any pecuniary losses which have been incurred as a result of the breach of contract. Compensation Only 8.13.8 In general, damages are compensatory in nature. It remains an open question whether, in the appropriate case, damages might be awarded for breach of contract on any other basis. Liquidated Compared with Unliquidated Damages 8.13.9 In some cases, compensation for losses resulting from breach may have been pre-agreed by the contracting parties as a term of the contract. If the agreed sum is a genuine pre-estimate of the loss which could be suffered as a result of a breach of the contract, the court will order that sum to be paid in compensation as liquidated damages. However, if the sum is intended to be a penalty aimed at ‘punishing’ the party-in-breach, the court will strike down the ‘penalty’ clause and award unliquidated damages instead to compensate the aggrieved party. Quantification and Measure of Unliquidated Damages 8.13.10 The court will usually quantify unliquidated damages so as to place the aggrieved party, as far as money can do so, in the position he or she would have been had the contract been performed fully. Therefore, if the aggrieved p
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the confidentiality obligations from 5 years to 20 years. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this may be unreasonably long and against public policy, especially if it restricts competition. The Law Explanation also states this could be challenged as an unreasonable restraint of trade, especially considering shorter limitation periods as per the Limitation Act (Cap. 163). Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions that courts may strike down unreasonable covenants. Therefore, extending the confidentiality period to 20 years can be viewed as creating an inconsistency and a legal contradiction, as it potentially violates public policy and principles related to restraint of trade.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of Singapore and shall be subject to the non -exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of Singapore.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of North Korea and shall be subject to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of North Korea.
perturbation.explanation: Choosing North Korean law and exclusive jurisdiction is problematic. It might be deemed unenforceable in Singapore or other jurisdictions due to public policy concerns, human rights issues, and the lack of a fair and impartial legal system in North Korea.
perturbation.law_citation: Common Law principles of Private International Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Singapore courts, under common law principles of private international law, will not enforce contractual provisions that are contrary to Singapore's public policy. Selecting North Korean law and exclusive jurisdiction could be considered contrary to public policy due to concerns about fairness, human rights, and the rule of law, making the governing law and jurisdiction clauses unenforceable in Singapore.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies Singapore law and jurisdiction, while the changed text designates North Korean law and exclusive jurisdiction. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation highlight that North Korean law and jurisdiction may be unenforceable in Singapore due to concerns about fairness, human rights, and the rule of law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, discussing 'conflict of laws,' indicates that a court must determine which jurisdiction's law to apply and that this determination is made in accordance with the law of the forum. Therefore, if a dispute arises in Singapore, a Singapore court might find the North Korean law clause unenforceable due to public policy concerns, creating a direct conflict and thus a legal contradiction. The shift to North Korean law directly undermines the original agreement's foundation under Singapore law.

perturbation.original_text: At any time requested in writing by Disclosing Party , the Receiving Party shall return or destroy all documents, samples or other tangible materials embodying Confidential Information, shall retain no copies thereof, and shall certify in writing that such destruction or return has been accomplished.
perturbation.changed_text: At any time requested in writing by Disclosing Party , the Receiving Party may return or destroy all documents, samples or other tangible materials embodying Confidential Information, shall retain copies thereof, and shall not be required to certify in writing that such destruction or return has been accomplished.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing the Receiving Party to retain copies of confidential information and removing the obligation to certify destruction or return creates a risk of misuse and violates the Disclosing Party's rights. This conflicts with general obligations of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singapore Contract Law - Implied Terms and Good Faith
perturbation.law_citation: Sembcorp Marine Ltd v PPL Holdings Pte Ltd [2013] 4 SLR 576
perturbation.law_explanation: While Singapore law does not have a codified duty of good faith, its presence is implied. Allowing retention and removing certification is likely to breach an implied term of good faith and fair dealing, which requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in the performance of their contractual obligations. The Sembcorp Marine Ltd case highlights how Singapore courts consider implied terms to prevent unfair or unreasonable outcomes.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: stare decisis | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you stare decisis Stare decisis is the doctrine that courts will adhere to precedent in making their decisions . Stare decisis means “to stand by things decided” in Latin. When a court faces a legal argument, if a previous court has ruled on the same or a closely related issue, then the court will make their decision in alignment with the previous court’s decision. The previous deciding-court must have binding authority over the court; otherwise, the previous decision is merely persuasive authority . In Kimble v. Marvel Enterprises , the U.S. Supreme Court described the rationale behind stare decisis as “promot[ing] the evenhanded, predictable, and consistent development of legal principles, foster[ing] reliance on judicial decisions, and contribut[ing] to the actual and perceived integrity of the judicial process.” The doctrine operates both horizontally and vertically. Horizontal stare decisis refers to a court adhering to its own precedent. For example, if the Seventh Circuit Court of Appeals adhered to the ruling of a previous Seventh Circuit Court of Appeals case, that would be horizontal stare decisis. A court engages in vertical stare decisis when it applies precedent from a higher court. For example, if the Seventh Circuit Court of Appeals adhered to a previous ruling from the U.S. Supreme Court, that would be vertical stare decisis. Or, additionally, if the Federal District Court for the Southern District of New York adhered to a previous ruling by the Second Circuit, that would be vertical stare decisis. Although courts seldom overrule precedent, the U.S. Supreme Court in Seminole Tribe of Florida v. Florida explained that stare decisis is not an “inexorable command.” When prior decisions are “unworkable or are badly reasoned,” then the Supreme Court may not follow precedent, and this is “particularly true in constitutional cases.” For example, in deciding Brown v. Board of Education , the U.S. Supreme Court explicitly renounced Plessy v. Ferguson , thereby refusing to apply the doctrine of stare decisis. [Last reviewed in December of 2021 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex ACADEMIC TOPICS legal history THE LEGAL PROCESS courts judicial administration wex definitions legal education and practice legal theory Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall return or destroy' to 'may return or destroy', combined with allowing the retention of copies and removing the certification requirement, directly undermines the original intent of confidentiality and secure handling of information. While the provided law snippet relates to 'stare decisis' and precedent, it indirectly relates to the principle that contracts should be predictable and consistent. The change introduces inconsistency regarding confidential information handling. The explanation points out the breach of implied term of good faith, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement does not create any partnership or agency relationship. This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the Parties on the subject matter hereof and supersede all prior agreements, communications and understandings of any nature what soever, oral or written. This Agreement may not be modified or waived orally and may be modified only in a writing signed by a duly authorized representative of both Parties .
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement does not create any partnership or agency relationship. This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the Parties on the subject matter hereof and supersede all prior agreements, communications and understandings of any nature what soever, oral or written. This Agreement may be modified or waived orally, and such oral modifications or waivers shall be binding even if not documented in writing.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing oral modifications contradicts the 'entire agreement' clause and the requirement for written modifications. This may be unenforceable, especially in sophisticated commercial agreements. Singapore law generally upholds 'no oral modification' clauses.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singapore Contract Law - Parol Evidence Rule
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 94 of the Evidence Act codifies the parol evidence rule, which generally prevents the introduction of oral evidence to contradict, vary, add to, or subtract from the terms of a written contract. Allowing oral modifications directly contradicts this rule and undermines the certainty and enforceability of written agreements. While there are exceptions to the rule, sophisticated parties in a commercial context are expected to adhere to written agreements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: evidence | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you evidence Evidence an item or information proffered to make the existence of a fact more or less probable. Evidence can take the form of testimony , documents , photographs, videos, voice recordings, DNA testing, or other tangible objects. Courts cannot admit all evidence, as evidence must be admissible under that jurisdiction’s rules of evidence (see below) in order to be presented to court. In federal court , evidence is governed by the Federal Rules of Evidence . A court may exclude evidence because it is not relevant , hearsay , or otherwise inadmissible . Rules of evidence are, as the name indicates, the rules by which a court determines what evidence is admissible at trial . In the U.S., federal courts follow the Federal Rules of Evidence , while state courts generally follow their own rules. See, for example California's evidence code, Indiana's evidence rules, or Washington's evidence rules. State rules of evidence are generally imposed by the state legislature upon the state courts. In establishing what evidence is admissible, many rules of evidence concentrate first on the relevancy of the offered evidence. See, for example Article IV of the Federal Rules of Evidence. The admissibility of hearsay and oral testimony are addressed under Article VIII of the Federal Rules of Evidence. Rules of evidence also allocate among the parties the burden of producing evidence and the burden of persuading the court. See, for example Article III of the Federal Rules of Evidence or Division 5 of the California Evidence Code. Privileges are addressed under Article V of the Federal Rules of Evidence as well as Division Eight of the California Evidence Code. perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the principle against introducing oral evidence to alter written agreements. The original contract explicitly prohibits oral modifications, reinforcing the written agreement's finality. The altered text then explicitly allows oral modifications, directly conflicting with the original written terms. The Law Explanation references Section 94 of the Evidence Act (the Parol Evidence Rule) which prevents oral evidence from contradicting a written agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 1 generally discusses evidence in court, but its description of evidence admissibility supports the idea that courts rely on rules of evidence to determine what is admissible, further supporting the importance of written contracts.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that any violation or threatened violation of this Agreement will ca use irreparable harm to Disclosing Party , entitling Disclosing Party to seek injunctive relief in addition to all other legal remedies.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that no violation or threatened violation of this Agreement will ca use irreparable harm to Disclosing Party , and Disclosing Party will not be entitled to seek injunctive relief, but shall only be limited to monetary damages.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the possibility of seeking injunctive relief limits the Disclosing Party's remedies in case of a breach, which contradicts established principles of contract law that allow for specific performance or injunctive relief where monetary damages are insufficient.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singapore Contract Law - Remedies for Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_explanation: While not explicitly mandating injunctive relief in every case, Singapore Contract Law recognizes it as a remedy when monetary damages are inadequate. Denying injunctive relief outright restricts the court's ability to provide appropriate redress and contradicts the principles underlying remedies for breach of contract. The Specific Relief Act allows for specific performance (which includes injunctions) in certain circumstances.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: specific performance | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you specific performance Specific performance is a contractual remedy in which a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations as closely as possible to what was promised in the contract , rather than simply paying damages for failing to do so. This remedy is often applied when monetary damages are inadequate to fix the harm caused by the breach ; most commonly in cases involving real property and rare chattels . Specific performance ensures that the aggrieved party receives exactly what was agreed upon in the contract, often in situations where the subject matter is unique or irreplaceable. [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS courts business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure commercial transactions courts and procedure property law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original contract allows the Disclosing Party to seek injunctive relief for violations, while the changed text explicitly prohibits it, limiting remedies to monetary damages. This contradicts the principle that specific performance (including injunctive relief) can be a remedy when monetary damages are inadequate, as supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states specific performance is applied when 'monetary damages are inadequate to fix the harm caused by the breach'. The changed text removes a potential remedy available to the Disclosing Party, creating a direct conflict with the circumstances under which injunctive relief is typically granted. The Law Explanation also supports this, stating that denying injunctive relief restricts the court's ability to provide appropriate redress.
